title: Rendering Pipeline
objectives: List the phases of a frame
Explain how a frame is scheduled and drawn
Connect rebuilds to layout, paint and raster

sections.markdown: The rendering pipeline is the sequence of phases Flutter runs each frame to turn your widget tree into pixels, driven by the vsync signal from the device's display.

sections.code:
vsync -> Animations/build -> LAYOUT -> PAINT -> COMPOSITE
      -> RASTERIZE (GPU) -> on screen
UI thread builds layers; Raster thread turns them into pixels.

rows: GPU converts layers to pixels (raster thread)

sections.markdown: Flutter targets one frame every ~16ms (60fps) or ~8ms (120fps). Work splits across the UI thread (build/layout/paint) and the raster thread (GPU). Jank happens when either thread overruns the frame budget.
Reduce per-frame work: minimize rebuilt subtrees (const, granular setState/selectors), use RepaintBoundary for animations, and avoid expensive paint (large blurs/shadows, saveLayer) in hot paths.

flashcards.back: Build → Layout → Paint → Composite → Rasterize, driven by vsync.
UI thread builds/layouts/paints (records layers); raster thread rasterizes layers to pixels on the GPU.
~16ms; exceed it on either thread and you get jank.

quiz.explain: Vsync from the display schedules frames.
The raster thread converts recorded layers into pixels via the GPU.
Build, then layout, then paint, composite, and rasterize.

challenges.text: Use the performance overlay to view UI vs raster thread timings.
Find a jank source with DevTools timeline and fix it.
Reduce rebuilds with const + a granular state selector and measure the difference.
Profile and eliminate a costly saveLayer/shadow in a scrolling list.

interviewQuestions: Walk through the rendering pipeline phases.
UI thread vs raster thread — what runs where?
What causes jank and how do you diagnose it?
How does vsync relate to frames?
How do you reduce per-frame work?
